segments.title: Inside Hikawa Maru's preserved engine room
segments.summary: A look below decks at Hikawa Maru's large diesel engines and the value of a well-preserved shipboard machinery space.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke begins below decks in the engine room of Hikawa Maru, where visitors can see the ship's large diesel engines closely. He emphasizes both the scale of the machinery and the care taken to preserve and display it. The moment is a useful reminder that a historic ship is more than its outward appearance: propulsion spaces explain how the vessel could make its voyages. For visitors interested in maritime technology, the accessible engine room turns an abstract description of diesel propulsion into a physical part of the ship's story.

evidence.note: He tours the engine room, describes the large diesel engines, and explains that the ship is well maintained for visitors.

segments.title: Hikawa Maru and Seattle's maritime connection
segments.summary: Hikawa Maru is presented as part of the shipping connection that linked Seattle to the wider world.
segments.body: The update places Hikawa Maru in the broader network of Pacific shipping rather than treating it only as a museum exhibit. Dr. Clarke repeats a local comparison in which the Great Northern helped found Seattle while the NYK Line acted as its mother. In that framing, ships such as Hikawa Maru connected Seattle with overseas markets and people and helped make that wider relationship practical. The watch point offers a concise entry into the importance of commercial shipping routes in the history of Pacific ports. It also explains why preserving a passenger ship can illuminate urban and international history as well as marine engineering.

evidence.note: He identifies the NYK Line as a link between Seattle and the wider world and says ships such as Hikawa Maru made that connection possible.
